items.q: Which type of nuclear radiation is most ionising?
items.correct: Alpha radiation — large, doubly charged particles cause the most ionisation per unit distance.
items.misconception: Gamma is most ionising because it can travel furthest and pass through almost anything.
items.random_wrong: Beta radiation is most ionising because it consists of fast electrons.
items.why: Students conflate penetrating power with ionising power — gamma penetrates most because it ionises least, the opposite of intuition.

items.q: What did Rutherford's alpha-scattering experiment show about the atom?
items.correct: Most of the atom is empty space, with a tiny dense positive nucleus.
items.misconception: It showed that atoms are solid spheres of positive charge with electrons embedded inside.
items.random_wrong: It showed that alpha particles can pass through any material because they are massless.
items.why: Students confuse the plum-pudding model (which Rutherford disproved) with the conclusion of his experiment.

items.q: What absorber is needed to stop beta radiation?
items.correct: A few millimetres of aluminium (or similar thin metal sheet).
items.misconception: A sheet of paper — beta is only slightly more penetrating than alpha.
items.random_wrong: Thick lead — only lead can stop any radiation.
items.why: Students remember 'paper, aluminium, lead' as a trio but mix up which goes with which radiation type.

items.q: Which pair are isotopes of the same element?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: U-235 and U-238. “C-14 and N-14” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: After alpha decay, the new nucleus has:
items.correct: Z reduced by 2, A reduced by 4
items.why: It's Z reduced by 2, A reduced by 4. “Same Z, A reduced by 4” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Radium ²²⁶₈₈Ra emits an alpha particle. What is the mass number A of the daughter nucleus?
items.why: The real figure is 222. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
